Contact Subjects: Science (General)
Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Richards covers the entire range of basic and applied science, including biomedical research and related consumer and policy issues. "Our audience is made up of general listeners across the country and beyond," she says. "So, stories need to transcend purely local, trade or insider interest." The show doesn't promote individual products or companies and doesn't do advocacy journalism. "The development, application, discovery or insight needs to be a genuine advance that has a real `so what?' for the general listener or for a large segment of the population," she says. "We avoid the incremental and the very preliminary." A media event such as a news conference or a launch isn't a news story, unless it contains a high news value. Contact Subjects: Children's education; Education; Higher education
Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Sanchez tracks national trends in grades K-12 and higher education. His coverage isn't limited to breaking news: "My stories weigh heavily on where education is going philosophically," he says. Don't send him an academic's Op-Ed piece that has already appeared in a major national newspaper. He wants clips from local media about school board politics or town-gown conflicts. Also pitch him on "school-to-work" issues--how schools are preparing students for the working world. Campus racial issues and Head Start programs interest him too. He advises PR pros representing schools to get a jump on controversial education stories by commenting before the press uncovers them. Sanchez reads mail and maintains a file on education experts. He typically files one story per week, but may spend up to two weeks on a single piece. Don't open a pitch by saying, "This is a story only NPR can do"--Sanchez doesn't want empty flattery. Mail a one-page letter with supporting local clips.

Contact Subjects: Business; Finance; Industry; Management; Personal finance
Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Covers the economy, markets and corporations. Pens consumer, personal finance and trend pieces as well as seasonal features. Responsible for "Consumer Watch," "Business Minute" and "Ag Minute." Catch his attention by recognizing slow news periods and fax pitches at those times; timeless stories are the best for the two-minute weekend features. "Put [the story] in context for me," he asks. Pitch three reasons why your company's product is national news and of interest to a broad audience. He is always looking for new financial analysts; fax him a letter listing the expert's name, area of expertise, credentials and direct phone number. "I don't want to go through someone else to reach them," he says. Sourcebooks, newsletters and reports from universities, hospitals and associations are also welcome. Fax breakers before 9 a.m.; anytime for weekend features. Brief calls (two minutes) are OK between 11 a.m. and early afternoon. Prefers to receive press releases, kits, product samples, backgrounders and story pitches. Guests should be able to speak in sound bites. Avoid "taking a lot of time. Give me a 20-second pitch. If it will work, I will know." "We tend to avoid business-to-business stories," he says, "because we think that tends to make the listeners' eyes gloss over. We prefer to focus on financial markets, economics and consumer or company news." For guests, in-studio CEOs and executives are always preferred, but if this is not possible, offer Hamrick a five- or 10-minute phone interview with your head honcho. "Your chances are improved if you offer us the highest company official that you have to offer," he says. "Think about what you might see in terms of business news stories in the Money section of USA Today or the front page of the Wall Street Journal." Tie your pitch to breaking news or an ongoing story and be candid about the facts. He's accustomed to dealing with sensitive information and will respect your wishes about when to go public. "I'd like to see more consumer-oriented pitches," Hamrick explains, and fewer stories that are "highly technical and not easily explained in 20 seconds." Pitch by email with a follow-up call. Best times to reach him are Tue.-Thu., 1-2:30 p.m.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Klinger works on the network's "All Things Considered" afternoon news program. "I work on hard news, but I also look for stories that are interesting, surprising and delightful," she says. "I'm not interested in how-to stories. The best PR pros understand the medium of radio and what we do. They have the radio on all the time. They call us when news breaks and offer experts who can comment on the subject. They pitch ideas to reporters at their local NPR affiliate who can include them in features they produce for us. Instead of emailing me, it's best to submit an idea on our website or call between 3 and 6 p.m. Eastern time to discuss it with me after asking me if I am on deadline," she says.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Raz hosts the weekend edition of "All Things Considered," which is primarily a news show and then a cultural program. "The thing that ties all the elements of the show together is that what we talk about is generally current or new," he says. "The focus is on stepping back from the news and taking a deeper look. We're not necessarily chained to the smallest news developments of the day. We're about looking at the biggest story of the day or week and breaking it down and drilling deep." In addition: "The best PR people understand the rhythm of the weekend show and how it's different from the weekday show," he says. Contact by email.

Outlet Profile: EDITORIAL PROFILE National Public Radio, the original publicly funded national radio network, is known for journalistic excellence and standard-setting news, information, and cultural programming.  NPR serves nearly 20 million listeners weekly via more than 680 NPR member stations.  Some programs are produced at NPR affiliates, such as WNYC and WHYY, and are then distributed nationwide by National Public Radio.  Other programs, such as  Living On Earth, are produced indepdently from NPR and its affiliates.  NPR also distributes programming to listeners in Europe, Asia, Australia, and Africa via NPR Worldwide, to military installations overseas via American Forces Network, and throughout Japan via cable.   National Public Radio has a large and loyal audience of listeners, who tend to be relatively educated and affluent.  NPR is staffed by seasoned reporters and producers, many of whom have won numerous awards.  Remember to focus on the big picture when pitching - business news stories need an angle that pulls the story out of the business world.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Based in Washington D.C., Mondello is a movie reviewer covering films nationally as soon as they open in New York and Los Angeles. "The best PR people make sure I'm aware of the picture early and can see the picture in Washington well in advance of it's opening in major markets," he says. "If it's not screening, they make sure that I get a DVD." He doesn't conduct interviews with actors or directors. "In the summer, I need to see films especially far in advance because I'm away for the entire month of July and the first part of August every year," he says. Provide him with screenings in May and early June for the period he'll be away. For example, Miramax sent him screenings about artist biographies, "Becoming Jane," "Moliere" and "Goya's Ghosts," which received substantial coverage. Reach him by email.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> DeRose oversees all economics coverage for the network. "The best thing PR people can do is pay attention to the news," he says. "If there's something big happening--like jobless numbers coming out on Friday--email me a few days before that and tell me, `These jobless numbers mean X, says so-and-so.'" Keep in mind: "NPR isn't interested in another new product or how-to book. Even nonfiction has a very high threshold for what we air on NPR. We're not looking for someone's 10 tips for doing X. It's much more analytical coverage. Pitches that say, `New book explains how to wrap Christmas presents better' have almost no chance of getting on."  His tips for PR: "There's tons of economic news coming out constantly. The stories that we look for involve real people as illustrations of larger trends. Often, when a PR person calls me and says, `I have this expert. He's writen a book on X,' I'll come back and say, `Can we talk to a real person involved in that situation?' Then, we might use the expert who's written the book. But the story will focus on the real experience of the person dealing with homelessness or unemployment--and then we'll use the expert as another voice in the piece, but not the sole voice in it."

Contact Subjects: Children's education; Education; Higher education
Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Sanchez tracks national trends in grades K-12 and higher education. His coverage isn't limited to breaking news: "My stories weigh heavily on where education is going philosophically," he says. Don't send him an academic's Op-Ed piece that has already appeared in a major national newspaper. He wants clips from local media about school board politics or town-gown conflicts. Also pitch him on "school-to-work" issues--how schools are preparing students for the working world. Campus racial issues and Head Start programs interest him too. He advises PR pros representing schools to get a jump on controversial education stories by commenting before the press uncovers them. Sanchez reads mail and maintains a file on education experts. He typically files one story per week, but may spend up to two weeks on a single piece. Don't open a pitch by saying, "This is a story only NPR can do"--Sanchez doesn't want empty flattery. Mail a one-page letter with supporting local clips.

"By establishing a working relationship in which you're helpful, no matter my inquiry, you're building the kind of trust that I will not only read your pitches, but seek you out from time-to-time," he says. Author pitches for new books work, especially if the subject matter has a broad American angle. "Most of my stories are text only, so I don't need a detailed interview with the author, just the book and sometimes, answers to a few specific questions," he says. Contact by email. Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Arnold covers among other things, the housing market and the sub-prime crisis. "Most of us are general audience-we don't want to hear about a computer chip that's ten percent faster," he says. "If your friends wouldn't be interested in your idea shared over the span of a few seconds, then radio listeners won't be either." He adds, "the idea has to be interesting to a sizeable chunk of the populace. A pitch about how hedge funds work won't grab us. But one about how hedge funds killed Bear Stearns might because it's about a huge bank taking, the search for blame and how that impacts everyday people's pocketbooks." Before pitching, conduct online research to figure out whom to contact. "If you can't answer questions toe-to-toe with us about the details your pitching, you will loss credibility," he says. "We'll find a way to get the information we need without you in the loop. Make sure you get briefed by the client or people in your company with the specifics before calling us." Make it through his tough filter by "not dong the salesman thing," he says. "By really being interested, knowledge about and, yes it's possible, even passionate about the pitch." In addition, be selective and "only call when it's really, really good," he says. "They're selective about what they call about and do so only when it's a perfect fit. They know we turn them off if they're always on machine-gun mode."
